note_data.title: Energy stores and transfers
keyPoints: **Energy stores** include kinetic, gravitational potential, elastic potential, chemical, nuclear, thermal and electrostatic
**Energy transfer** occurs by heating, radiation, forces doing work and electrical working
**Conservation of energy** states that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transferred from one store to another
**Kinetic energy** is the energy stored in moving objects and equals ½mv²
**Gravitational potential energy** is energy stored in objects at height and equals mgh
**Elastic potential energy** is energy stored in stretched or compressed objects
**Chemical energy** is energy stored in food, fuels and batteries
**Work done** equals force × distance moved in direction of force
**Power** is the rate of energy transfer and equals work done ÷ time taken
Energy is measured in **joules (J)** and power is measured in **watts (W)**

definitions.definition: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transferred from one store to another
The energy transferred when a force moves an object through a distance
The rate of energy transfer or rate of doing work
The fraction of energy input that is usefully transferred

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show working clearly with correct formula, substitution and unit
Give a sequence of points about what happens

modelAnswer.question: A ball of mass 0.5 kg is dropped from a height of 10 m. Calculate the kinetic energy of the ball just before it hits the ground. (g = 10 m/s²)
modelAnswer.answer: GPE = mgh = 0.5 × 10 × 10 = 50 J. By conservation of energy, all gravitational potential energy converts to kinetic energy. Therefore KE = 50 J
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Correct use of GPE = mgh
Mark 3: Stating conservation of energy or KE = 50 J

commonMistakes: Never say 'energy is lost' — always say 'energy is transferred to thermal energy store'
Students often forget to include units (J for energy, W for power)
Confusing power and energy — power is rate of energy transfer, not amount of energy
Writing 'energy is destroyed' instead of 'energy is transferred to less useful stores'
Forgetting to square the velocity in KE = ½mv²
examinerTips: Energy calculations appear in almost every paper — practice substitution into formulas
Always start energy transfer questions by identifying initial and final energy stores
Sankey diagrams often test energy efficiency — practice drawing these
Use conservation of energy principle to solve problems involving energy transfers
formulas: Efficiency = useful energy output ÷ total energy input
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Sankey diagram showing energy input on left, useful energy output at top right, and wasted energy (usually thermal) at bottom right. Arrow thickness represents amount of energy.
